EMBEzZLEMENT CHARGE.


NJ. j. Gordon, successfully a
criminal lawyer, politician, supreme
court justice, and counsel for the
Great Northern, is on trial charged
with embezzling $4,000 placed in
his hands by the railroad company
to satisfy judgment awarded to
James S. Sparrow, a railway mail
clerk who was fatally injured in a
train wreck on July 81, 1907, at
Great Falls, Mont.


While the embezZlement charge
is the basis of the present trial, Cor
don is charged with corrupting JUS
tees of the supreme court to secure
decisions favorable to the Great
Northern. His connection with JUS
tice Milo A. Root in a case in which
Gordon had drafted the text of a
| decision favorable to the railroad,
caused the resignation of Root and
| much newspaper comment in 1908.
During the summer of 1908 Cor
don resigned from his position as
counsel for the Great Northern, l..
c. Gilman of Seattle. general west
ern counsel for the railroad, &C
/ knOwledging that Gordon was short
in his accounts in the neighborhoOd
of 100000.


Eight indictments were found
against Gordon by the 1909 grand
| jury. seven charging him with en-
| beZzlement and one with drawing a
/ check on a bank where he had no
funds.


JURORS EXAMINED.


At the noon recess 13 jurors had
| been examined, three of whom were
excused. Those excused were J. w.
G. Hanford. NJ. Kulp and G.. H.
Brownlee. Those temporarily
passed were John c. Glass. E. C.
DuntOn. l.. Larsen, Albert Bradley.
c. E. Brandt, Thomas Meager, w.
H. Kuns, J. II. Blake. George E.
Darby and w. A. Walker.


GORDON CONFIDENT.


Defendant Gordon wears a look
of supreme confidence. spending his
time with his attorneys and atten-
lively following the examinations.


Prosecutor Fred c. Pugh, with
Deputies Don F. Kizer and D. W.
Hurn, are prosecuting the case,
while Nuzum & Nuzum. Frank
Graves and Potter Charles Sullivan,
of Seattle, brother Of the Judge, rep
resent the defendant.
